name: Ramp Race: Exploring Acceleration
description: Roll different objects down ramps of varying angles and surfaces. Predict which object will reach the bottom first and why. Measure distances and times to calculate speeds. Discover that steeper ramps produce greater acceleration and that mass does not affect the rate of rolling (ignoring air resistance). Connect observations to the concept of acceleration as a change in velocity over time.
materials: Books or blocks to prop up one end
learning_objectives: Define acceleration as the rate of change of velocity
Predict how ramp angle affects acceleration
Measure distance and time to calculate average speed
Create position-time and velocity-time graphs from data
variations: K-2: Simply race objects down ramps and describe fast/slow without measuring
9-12: Calculate theoretical acceleration using a = g * sin(theta) and compare to measured values
Add different surfaces (sandpaper, cloth, smooth) to explore friction effects

name: Marble Momentum Collisions
description: Set up marble collisions on a flat surface using a ruler track or grooved channel. Roll one marble into a stationary marble and observe what happens. Try collisions between marbles of different sizes. Measure speeds before and after collisions. Discover that momentum (mass times velocity) is conserved: what the first marble loses, the second marble gains. Explore elastic vs. inelastic collisions.
materials: Marbles of at least two different sizes
Ruler with groove or cardboard channel
learning_objectives: Define momentum as mass times velocity
Observe conservation of momentum in collisions
Distinguish elastic and inelastic collisions
Predict collision outcomes based on relative masses
variations: 3-4: Use toy cars on a flat surface for larger, slower collisions that are easier to observe
11-12: Calculate momentum before and after collisions numerically and verify conservation law
Explore 2D collisions by rolling marbles at angles

name: Rubber Band Car Energy Conversion
description: Build a simple car from a cardboard box, pencils for axles, and bottle caps for wheels, powered by a rubber band wound around the rear axle. Wind the rubber band different numbers of turns and measure how far the car travels each time. Trace the energy transformations: elastic potential energy (stored in the wound rubber band) converts to kinetic energy (car moving) and then to thermal energy (friction stops the car). Plot distance traveled vs. number of winds.
materials: Small cardboard box or milk carton
learning_objectives: Identify elastic potential energy stored in a rubber band
Trace energy transformations from potential to kinetic to thermal
Apply conservation of energy: total energy is constant, just changing form
Graph the relationship between stored energy and distance traveled
variations: K-2: Simply wind and release, observing that more winds means further travel
9-12: Calculate elastic potential energy using E = 0.5 * k * x^2 and compare to measured kinetic energy
Design challenge: build the car that travels the farthest on a single rubber band

name: Heat Transfer Investigation
description: Place spoons made of different materials (metal, wood, plastic) in a cup of hot water. Dab a small amount of butter or margarine on the handle end of each spoon. Time how long it takes for the butter to melt on each spoon. The metal spoon transfers heat fastest (high thermal conductivity), wood is slowest. Discuss conduction as heat transfer through direct contact. Extend to convection (hot water rising) and radiation (feeling heat from a lamp without touching it).
materials: Cup of hot water (adult supervision for younger learners)
Small dabs of butter or margarine
learning_objectives: Define conduction as heat transfer through direct contact
Compare thermal conductivity of different materials
Distinguish conduction, convection, and radiation
Predict which materials make good insulators vs. conductors
variations: K-3: Simply feel which spoon handle gets warm first (no butter needed)
9-12: Measure temperature at handle over time and calculate thermal conductivity
Design challenge: build the best insulated container to keep ice from melting

name: Slinky Wave Explorer
description: Stretch a Slinky along the floor between two people. Create transverse waves by moving one end side-to-side. Create longitudinal waves by pushing and pulling one end along the length. Measure wavelength (distance between two crests) and count how many waves pass a point in 10 seconds to find frequency. Discover the wave equation: speed equals frequency times wavelength. Explore how tension (stretching the Slinky more) affects wave speed.
materials: Metal Slinky (metal transmits waves better than plastic)
Tape or chalk to mark positions
learning_objectives: Distinguish transverse and longitudinal waves
Measure wavelength and frequency
Discover the wave equation: v = f * lambda
Observe how changing tension affects wave speed
variations: K-2: Simply create waves and describe them as fast/slow, big/small
9-12: Calculate wave speed quantitatively and verify v = f * lambda
Create standing waves by shaking at just the right frequency

name: Build a Pinhole Camera
description: Construct a simple camera obscura from a small box (shoe box works well). Cut a small square hole in one end and cover it with aluminum foil. Poke a tiny hole in the foil with a pin. Cover the other end with wax paper or tracing paper (the screen). In a dimly lit room, point the pinhole at a bright object (a lamp or window). Observe the inverted image on the screen. Explain using the principle that light travels in straight lines: light from the top of the object passes through the pinhole and hits the bottom of the screen, and vice versa.
materials: Shoe box or similar small box
Dark cloth or towel (to reduce stray light)
learning_objectives: Demonstrate that light travels in straight lines
Explain why the pinhole camera produces an inverted image
Relate pinhole size to image brightness and sharpness
Connect the pinhole camera principle to how real cameras and eyes work
variations: K-3: Use a pre-made pinhole camera and simply observe the inverted image
9-12: Vary pinhole diameter and measure image sharpness; discuss diffraction limits
Build a room-sized camera obscura by darkening a room and making a pinhole in window covering

name: Simple Circuit Builder
description: Use batteries, small light bulbs (or LEDs), and wires to build working circuits. Start with a single bulb and battery. Add a switch. Then build series circuits (bulbs in a line) and parallel circuits (bulbs on separate branches). Predict what happens when you unscrew one bulb in each configuration. Discover that in series circuits, removing one bulb breaks the whole circuit, while in parallel circuits the other bulbs stay lit. Discuss why houses use parallel wiring.
materials: Small switches (or use paper clips as switches)
Paper and pencil for drawing circuit diagrams
learning_objectives: Build a complete circuit with battery, wire, and load
Distinguish series and parallel circuit configurations
Predict circuit behavior when components are added or removed
Draw simple circuit diagrams using standard symbols
variations: K-2: Use snap-circuit kits for easier assembly
9-12: Measure voltage and current with a multimeter; verify Ohm's law V = IR
Design challenge: wire a model house with working lights and switches

name: Electromagnet Strength Challenge
description: Wrap insulated wire around a large iron nail to create an electromagnet. Connect the wire ends to a battery. Test the electromagnet by picking up paperclips and counting how many it can hold. Then systematically vary one factor at a time: number of wire coils, number of batteries (voltage), and nail material. Graph paperclips lifted vs. each variable. Discover that more coils and more voltage both increase magnetic strength. Discuss how electromagnets are used in real applications (doorbells, maglev trains, MRI machines).
materials: Large iron nail (6 inch or longer)
learning_objectives: Build a working electromagnet and test its strength
Identify variables that affect electromagnetic strength
Design and conduct a controlled experiment (changing one variable at a time)
Connect electromagnets to real-world applications
variations: K-3: Simply build the electromagnet and observe that it picks up paperclips when connected
9-12: Calculate magnetic field strength B = mu_0 * n * I and compare to measured results
Compare permanent magnets to electromagnets: what are advantages of each?

name: Roller Coaster Energy Audit
description: Build a marble roller coaster from foam pipe insulation tubing (cut in half lengthwise) and tape. Design at least three hills and one loop. Before building, predict the maximum height of the second hill based on the first hill (it must be lower due to energy loss from friction). Roll a marble from the top and observe which hills it clears. Measure the heights and track where energy is lost. Create an energy bar chart showing gravitational potential energy, kinetic energy, and thermal energy (friction losses) at each point along the track.
materials: Books or boxes for elevation support
Pencil and paper for energy diagrams
learning_objectives: Apply conservation of energy to predict roller coaster behavior
Explain why each successive hill must be shorter than the previous one
Create energy bar charts showing energy transformation at different positions
Identify where friction converts kinetic energy to thermal energy
variations: K-4: Build and observe without measuring; describe which hills the marble clears and why
9-12: Calculate velocities at each point using mgh = 0.5mv^2 and compare to timed measurements
Design challenge: build a coaster that completes a loop using the minimum starting height

name: Half-Life Coin Simulation
description: Model radioactive decay using 100 coins. Each coin represents one radioactive atom. Shake all coins in a container and pour them out. Remove every coin showing heads (these atoms have decayed). Count and record the remaining coins. Repeat until all coins have been removed. Plot the number of remaining coins after each round. The resulting curve shows exponential decay with a half-life of one round (since roughly half the coins show heads each time). Compare to real radioactive decay curves and discuss how scientists use half-life to date archaeological and geological samples.
learning_objectives: Model radioactive decay as a random process
Define half-life as the time for half the atoms to decay
Create and interpret an exponential decay graph
Connect half-life to real-world applications like carbon dating
variations: 6: Use 50 coins for a quicker simulation with the same pattern
11-12: Derive the decay equation N = N_0 * (1/2)^(t/half-life) from the data
Compare results from multiple groups to show statistical variation in small samples
